title: CEO Interview with Yossi Meyouhas of Xsight Labs - Semiwiki
gist: Xsight Labs CEO Yossi Meyouhas claims its fully software-defined, parallel (non-recirculating) switch/DPU architecture eliminates the traditional programmability-vs-performance trade-off in networking silicon, shipping the E1-SoC — the first fully software-defined 800G DPU with 64 Arm Neoverse N2 cores inline giving roughly 4x competitors' inline CPU performance — and is positioning against vendor lock-in via open ISA/SDKs.

insights.claim: Traditional networking silicon forces a trade-off between programmability and performance: programmable switches recirculate packets to handle complex logic (killing throughput and adding latency), while fixed-function chips are fast but require waiting for the next hardware generation for new features.
insights.speaker: Yossi Meyouhas
insights.so_what: Defines the core competitive axis in networking silicon (programmability vs. performance) that Xsight claims to have broken — a framework for evaluating rival DPU/switch vendors' architectural trade-offs.
insights.quote: There was a fundamental trade-off in networking silicon today: you can have programmability or you can have performance, but not both. Programmable switches recirculate packets to handle complex logic, which kills throughput and adds latency.

insights.claim: Xsight Labs' architecture executes in parallel rather than through serial pipelines, enabling full programmability at line rate without recirculation penalties, so customers can add new protocols, security functions, or telemetry features in software deployed in weeks rather than years.
insights.speaker: Yossi Meyouhas
insights.so_what: If true, compresses feature deployment cycles in data-center networking from years to weeks, a potentially disruptive speed advantage versus fixed-function incumbents' hardware-generation release cadence.
insights.quote: Our architecture executes in parallel rather than through serial pipelines, so you get full programmability at line rate without recirculation penalties.

insights.claim: Xsight shipped the E1-SoC, described as the first fully software-defined 800G DPU, with 64 Arm Neoverse N2 cores placed directly in the data path (not bolted on for exception handling as competitors do), giving roughly 4x the inline CPU performance of competing designs.
insights.speaker: Yossi Meyouhas
insights.so_what: A concrete, named product (E1-SoC, 800G, 64 Neoverse N2 cores, ~4x inline CPU performance) that investors/customers can benchmark directly against incumbent DPU vendors' specs.
insights.quote: We shipped the E1-SoC, the first fully software-defined 800G DPU. It has 64 Arm Neoverse N2 cores directly in the data path, not bolted on for exception handling like competitors do. That gives roughly 4x the inline CPU performance.

themes.theme: AI cluster networking and vendor lock-in as customer pain points

insights.claim: In AI training clusters, traffic is extremely bursty, and Xsight's fully shared buffer architecture absorbs microbursts that would otherwise cause packet drops and tail-latency spikes on conventional switches, which matters because in collective operations the slowest link determines overall training speed.
insights.speaker: Yossi Meyouhas
insights.so_what: Identifies microburst-induced tail latency as a direct bottleneck on AI training cluster throughput — a mechanism investors can use to evaluate networking silicon claims across the AI infrastructure stack.
insights.quote: That matters because in collective operations, the slowest link determines overall training speed.
insights.locator: application areas, AI and cloud networking fabrics

insights.claim: Customers worry most about time-to-market and vendor lock-in: missing a deployment window by months means a competitor's AI cluster is already training the next model, and being dependent on one vendor's proprietary SDK costs customers leverage on pricing and feature priorities.
insights.speaker: Yossi Meyouhas
insights.so_what: Highlights that AI infrastructure deployment cycles have compressed to the point where a few months' delay is competitively fatal — a useful benchmark for assessing urgency in hyperscaler procurement decisions.
insights.quote: Missing a window by a few months means your competitor’s cluster is already training the next model.
insights.locator: what keeps your customers up at night

insights.claim: Xsight differentiates on openness by using open standards and providing a fully open instruction set architecture and SDKs, letting customers own their software investment and switch, extend, or customize without vendor permission, in contrast to incumbents' closed, fixed-function silicon.
insights.speaker: Yossi Meyouhas
insights.so_what: An open-ISA go-to-market strategy directly targets the lock-in pain point of closed incumbent DPU/switch vendors — worth watching whether this becomes a broader industry trend pressuring proprietary SDK models.
insights.quote: We use open standards and provide a fully open instruction set architecture, so customers own their software investment. They can switch, extend, or customize without asking permission.
insights.locator: what keeps your customers up at night

insights.claim: Xsight is expanding into deep space communications, applying its high-throughput parallel processing architecture to handle traffic under radiation and extreme environmental constraints, calling it a technically natural fit despite being a very different market.
insights.speaker: Yossi Meyouhas
insights.so_what: An unusual adjacent-market expansion (deep space comms) signals the underlying architecture's claimed generality beyond data-center networking — a minor diversification data point for tracking Xsight's business scope.
insights.quote: We’re also expanding into deep space communications, where our processing architecture handles high-throughput traffic under radiation and extreme environmental constraints, a very different market, but technically a natural fit.
